Please see the important performance and other related disclosures at the end of this Commentary, which are an integral part of this quarterly Commentary Newsletter.

In the second quarter, there were double-digit returns for equities as global economic activity measures lifted into expansion territory. However, there were many tremors across the economic landscape, with Washington DC at the epicenter.

  • At the start of April, the U.S.’s proposed tariff regime led to significant economic fallout. Global markets whipsawed in the ensuing tariff tantrum and subsequent de-escalations to the point that later tariff announcements were often discounted quickly.
  • Markets showed their concerns as U.S. treasury yields rose, and the dollar fell against major currencies.

Equities for now seemed unfazed as the U.S.’s Russell 3000® Index rose 11%, keeping pace with the 12% returns for the MSCI EAFE and MSCI Emerging Markets Indexes.

  • Growth indexes outpaced value indexes in all regions and size ranges.
  • Among global risk factors, Beta, Momentum, and Residual Volatility were highly rewarded, while Earnings Quality, Value, and Growth were punished or ignored.

As our investment team meets with companies, dissects recent earnings reports, and reviews the global landscape, they note several investment dynamics in various market segments that guide our positioning:

In the U.S.:

  • Companies across most areas either scrap or significantly reduced forward guidance: As companies wait to see how near-term results fare in this environment, we believe some have lowered expectations to levels that can be surpassed in future quarters.
  • Consumer: With so many consumer goods potentially subject to tariffs, measures of confidence and sentiment remained at trough levels. Some customers advanced their purchases ahead of expected price hikes.
  • Financials: Although M&A activity began unexpectedly slowly this year, there were early signs it was accelerating.
  • Health Care: Reduced Medicaid spending weighed on volume levels and growth across the sector. Changes at the FDA slowed regulatory approvals, which combined with the Trump administration’s rhetoric for lower drug prices and onshore production, have dampened enthusiasm for the biopharma industry.
  • Industrials: Many companies are understandably conservative with their outlooks as tariff levies are quickly announced and rescinded. That favored longer-cycle secular end markets—such as aerospace & defense, datacenter-related, and infrastructure—compared to the short-cycle cyclical areas of transportation or consumer-related.
  • Information Technology: As with other segments, anticipation of higher prices from tariffs led to a short-term surge of orders across the semiconductor industry. Offsetting that was the rebound in spending for all things AI-related, which had been weak in the first quarter following DeepSeek’s emergence.

In Europe:

  • Decoupling: in addition to previously discussed energy decoupling from Russia, defense decoupling from the U.S. also became a central topic. Trade decoupling, and further down the line, technology decoupling is also shaping out to be a structural growth driver, potentially driving reshoring activities across the continent.
  • The softening of regulations: Excessive regulation hampered European growth in the past many years. The urgency of energy independence has prompted another look at the region’s forced march towards carbon neutrality, for example. With its decidedly "pro-growth" stance, the new German government's commitment to economic investment is expected to generate positive ripple effects throughout Europe.
  • ECB: With inflation under control, a strong Euro, and a weak GDP, a rate cutting cycle can persist.

In Japan:

  • Continued corporate reforms are having an impact: Japanese corporate governance reforms are finally yielding more sustainable and tangible impacts on the Japanese stock market, with companies prioritizing efficient capital allocation. This is evident in record high dividend increases and substantial share buybacks.
  • Inflation, Interest Rates, and Trade: The BOJ and Japanese government face a tricky environment related to inflation, interest rates and global trades, where a gradual strengthening of the yen appears to be the easiest course. While a stronger currency presents a headwind for the export sector, it concurrently creates opportunities for domestic-facing firms.
  • Continued focus on domestic upgrading and investment: Japan continues to heavily invest in digital transformation, infrastructure upgrades, and workforce productivity. These investments are particularly incentivized by challenges related to labor recruitment and talent shortages.

The Global Small Cap Portfolio outperformed the MSCI World Small Cap benchmark in the second quarter of 2025. This outperformance was supported by positive allocation effect and strong stock selection across most regions.

Regional Performance: The Americas

The Americas was the portfolio’s largest regional contributor supported by strong stock selection.

Many of our Industrials positions provide necessary business-to-business operational services, highly technical components, equipment enabling automation & efficiency improvements, or essential infrastructure services. Contributing a 45% return was EMCOR Group (EME), which provides construction and operational services for mechanical and electrical systems to a broad range of commercial, industrial, utility, and institutional customers. EMCOR reported revenues and earnings that bested expectations with a record high level of remaining performance obligations (outstanding and unbilled work for existing customers) along with a healthy pipeline of new projects. Regal Rexnord Corp. (RRX) manufactures automation sub-systems, industrial powertrain solutions, power transmission components, and air moving products. Its share price rose 30% on the heels of its first quarter, which stood in stark contrast to the market's lowered expectations and a reduced outlook. Most of the revenue upside came from the Power Efficiency Solutions segment. Management reiterated forward guidance as they have not seen any evidence of a demand slowdown and believe they are well positioned to navigate the impact of tariffs. Loar Holdings (LOAR), a diversified manufacturer and supplier of niche aerospace and defense components, saw its share price climb 23%. At the start of the quarter, increased production plans for new Boeing aircraft left many parts manufacturers scrambling, which in turn increased demand for Loar’s components. Its share rose and we trimmed our position. Later the company reported better-than-expected revenues though earnings were merely as anticipated. Loar’s overall margins were pinched by a higher proportion of defense sales and additional internal costs since becoming publicly traded last year.

Among the wide variety of Information Technology companies, we prefer critical system providers, specialized component designers, systems that improve productivity or efficiency for their clients, and others that are growing their shares of corporate IT budgets. JFrog (FROG) provides systems to manage and speed up the release of software updates from developers to users. We added to our position on near-term lows at the start of the quarter. Later, its reported revenues and earnings surpassed expectations, and JFrog’s management lifted its prior guidance for 2025. In addition to a stable level of new business wins, there were levels of customer usage beyond their contractual commitments that also boosted revenues. That lifted its shares up by 40% and we trimmed the position. Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) offers programmable logic semiconductor products, system solutions, design services, and licenses. The company's first-quarter results and second-quarter guidance both came in line with Street estimates. We believe Lattice is well-positioned to benefit from new product ramps, particularly with its Avant platform, which is expected to gain significant traction in the second half of 2025. Its diverse revenue streams across computing, communications, automotive, and industrial applications provide resilience against cyclical downturns in specific sectors. Moreover, strength in the data center segment is driving growth, helping to mitigate potential negative impacts from fluctuations in other markets. We added to our position as its share price was down -4% in the quarter. Onto Innovation Inc. (ONTO) designs, develops, and produces high-performance control metrology, defect inspection, lithography, and data analysis systems that are used by microelectronic device manufacturers. While first quarter results were in line with the Street, second quarter projections fell short. That triggered a -13% tumble in its share price. Workiva Inc. (WK) offers cloud-based compliance and regulatory reporting solutions. Results for the latest quarter edged estimates and the company maintained full year revenue guidance, thereby reflecting continued momentum in multi-solution platform deals. Management did however indicate that new business bookings could be impacted by a less favorable buying environment. These cautionary comments served to pressure the stock price by -9%. Synaptics (SYNA), a developer of human interface semiconductor solutions for electronic devices, faced persistent delays in its recovery, with estimates continually revised downward. The anticipated Windows 10 phase-out catalyst has thus far failed to materialize. Given Synaptics' status as a consumer-exposed semiconductor company in the portfolio, impacting highly discretionary product categories, we liquidated our position, which lost -25% this quarter.

Our preferences among Health Care stocks are those companies providing novel therapies for unmet needs that deserve premium pricing, or specialized service providers. Inspire Medical Systems Inc. (INSP), a medical technology company focused on the development and commercialization of innovative solutions for treating obstructive sleep apnea, pulled back by -18%. First quarter revenues exceeded consensus estimates. That report was overshadowed by second quarter guidance, which was viewed as disappointing. Management explained the second quarter will be a transitional period as centers work through their Inspire IV system inventory ahead of ordering the recently launched Inspire V systems.

Within the real estate sector, National Storage Affiliates (NSA), a U.S. self-storage facility operator, saw its share price decline -16%. The company reported disappointing first quarter results, though management reiterated its full-year guidance.

Canada-based Pason Systems (OTCQX:PSYTF), a designer and producer of sensors, instrumentation, and data management systems for land-based oil and gas drilling rigs, was down -2%. Despite reporting better-than-expected first-quarter results, the macro environment remained murky. Management, however, expected any industry activity decrease to be more modest in both depth and duration than prior industry slowdowns.

Regional Performance: Europe

A growing awareness in Europe points towards the need to decouple from foreign influences across various sectors. Following past discussions on energy independence and more recently on defense, concerns about tariffs are now highlighting the need for trade decoupling. Looking ahead, we anticipate a medium-term objective of greater European technology self-determination, viewing infrastructure like the cloud as strategic assets.

Case in point is the portfolio’s top contributor for two consecutive quarters, Renk Group (OTCPK:RNKGF), with its 53% surge. This German company is a global leader in mission-critical drive technology for land and naval defense applications, with civilian products used in marine, industrial, and energy transition sectors. During the NATO budget meeting on June 25th, the group declared its intention to move defense budget targets to 3.5% (+1.5% for adjacent spending). With this announcement, we believe much of the anticipated upside in the aerospace and defense sector has materialized, leading us to significantly reduce our exposure and take profits.

Within the information technology sector, the IT services industry is largely insulated from direct tariff impacts. However, it is highly cyclical since IT budgets are closely tied to corporate revenue growth and overall business confidence. France-based Sopra Steria (OTCPK:SPSAF) saw its share price ascend 30%. They offer a comprehensive suite of services including consulting, systems integration, software development, infrastructure management, and business process services. We increased our position in Sopra Steria during the first quarter on price weakness, anticipating that the challenging spending environment in Europe would be temporary and that revenue trends would begin to improve in the second half of 2025. Additionally, Sopra Steria is well-positioned to directly benefit from increased spending in the European defense and security industries due to its significant exposure to this space.

Regional Performance: Japan

Our Japanese holdings outperformed the benchmark's country return. Japan is currently in a "wait and see" mode, as tariff uncertainty and an upcoming election appear to be delaying a BOJ rate hike despite inflationary pressures. We maintain a balanced portfolio of leading businesses across diverse sectors, making it less sensitive, though not immune, to macroeconomic factors like FX, interest rates, and tariffs.

We see the start of a possible shift of focus away from inbound spending to the areas where local consumers play a bigger role. Food & Life Companies (OTCPK:SGLOF), Japan’s leading quick-service sushi chain, was a beneficiary of the strong growth in inbound traffic as well as local consumptions. The company reported another strong month of sales growth, which drove its share price 64% higher. The faster-than-expected rollout of their digital conveyor belt system, Digiro, has also boosted per-customer spending. We anticipate this positive trend will continue, favorably impacting margins.

Azbil (OTCPK:YMATF), a Japanese leader in HVAC systems for buildings, supplies a wide range of building automation products and services. The company is benefiting from increased demand for building automation driven by higher energy prices, rising temperatures, and stricter building regulations. They have also seen rapid growth in their data center business across Japan and Southeast Asia. Despite management issuing a conservative outlook and maintaining FY27 medium-term ROE and margin targets—even after achieving FY24 goals—Azbil's more aggressive share buyback plan and higher dividend payout reflect an improved capital allocation mindset. Its share price increased 20%.

A leading systems developer and business consultant, Simplex Holdings, reported inline quarterly results, demonstrating a notable earnings recovery, and its share price jumped 43%. Strong growth in Strategic DX Consulting led the company to increase its gross margin guidance over the next two years. With a positive demand environment, the company is also showing robust order and backlog growth.

Regional Performance: Emerging Markets

The portfolio’s off-benchmark Emerging Markets holdings significantly outperformed the benchmark’s overall performance.

Arcos Dorados (ARCO), the largest independent McDonald's franchisee with over 2,000 restaurants across 20 Latin American and Caribbean countries, reported a slight revenue beat but missed on margin and EBITDA. Management indicated stronger trends in April, notably in Mexico, which was most affected by Holy Week and should see the strongest rebound in the second quarter. Despite a later rebound, the stock ended the quarter down -1%.

HD Hyundai Marine Solution, the maintenance and repair unit of South Korea's largest shipbuilding conglomerate, HD Hyundai Heavy Group, reported better-than-expected results, underscored by significant operating leverage potential in both the after-market and the retrofit businesses. Hyundai Marine also benefits from its position as a non-Chinese ship builder, which insulates it from tariff impacts. We exited the position on strong performance with its share price up 49% while it was held during the quarter.

Conclusion

Markets and economies swung wildly in the first half of 2025, generally propelled by geopolitical drivers—most of those emanating from the U.S. Many companies downplayed their outlooks either in response or in advance of tariff-induced cost increases. Yet markets have a remarkable way of absorbing those concerns, pricing them in, and forecasting improvements. For example, most Federal Reserve watchers anticipate a rate reduction later this year. However, many investors are cautious regarding the intended and unintended consequences of the newly enacted massive tax and spending bill. We seek to balance our holdings between companies that should be resilient to potential headwinds and those poised to catch any accelerating tailwinds in their sails. Either way, our focus remains on the bottom line, seeking companies that are better equipped to maintain their fundamentals when peers are deteriorating, or to lead others with faster growth. As always, we are available for any questions you might have.

data.title: Wal-Mart De Mexico: Taking Stock After The Big Selloff
data.description:
Wal-Mart de México, S.A.B. de C.V., or Walmex (OTCPK:WMMVF), currently 71% owned by Walmart (WMT), is the largest retailer in Mexico, which accounts for >80% of its total revenues. The Walmex store portfolio covers virtually all Mexican socioeconomic levels; its main format is budget-friendly Bodegas Aurrera, followed by full-service Walmart hyper/supermarkets for the middle- to upper-middle-class, as well as membership warehouse clubs (Sam's Club) for businesses and households.

Like its US parent, Walmex has also leveraged its retail dominance into an ecosystem, albeit a broader one, spanning e-commerce (in-store pickup and delivery), low-cost mobile (Bait), digital finance (Cashi), and advertising (Walmart Connect).

As I’ve highlighted in my past coverage (see Wal-Mart de Mexico: Well Equipped To Weather The Cycles), the Walmex model works well and has driven impressive ‘compounder’ type stock price returns.

Not so much this year, though, as shares have significantly underperformed broader Mexican indices. This month’s quarterly earnings miss certainly didn’t help things, nor have the macro headwinds inside and outside of Mexico. Still, with the stock now priced at a larger than ever discount to WMT (vs. its historical P/E premium), Walmex is worth a closer look.

Growing Ahead of the Industry

Walmex stock may have been pummeled post-earnings, but at first glance, its top-line momentum in Q2 offered some positives. To recap, same-store sales growth actually accelerated to +4.4% in Mexico and to +4.0% (constant currency) in Central America. This outpaced same-store sales growth in Mexican retail, as tracked by ANTAD, by a very solid (albeit narrowed) +80bps gap. Growth was also notably positive across all its major formats and regions.

A look beneath the headline same store sales print, however, showed that there were some caveats. Per management on the post-earnings Q&A call, Mexico traffic was actually down -1.4% for the quarter, with ticket growth (+6.0%) making up the shortfall. Like in Q1, the beverage category was the key drag for traffic. Meanwhile, Central American same-store sales were helped by positive contributions from both traffic (+1.4%) and ticket growth (+2.6%).

Another notable was that total store sales growth (i.e., including new store contributions) paced at +6.0% in both Mexico and Central America. While strong, Walmex only added 20 new stores in Mexico in Q2 and five in Central America. This does bring its overall store footprint to a region-leading 4.1k, though this run-rate is well below its outlined 1.5k new store target through 2029.

As for Walmex's e-commerce gross merchandise value (or GMV) in Mexico, growth accelerated to +20% YoY (on-demand +24%, marketplace +14%). While growth here is coming from a lower base vs. the core brick & mortar business, this acceleration is positive in the face of stiff competition from the likes of MercadoLibre (MELI), Amazon (AMZN), and El Puerto de Liverpool (OTCPK:ELPQF). Ultimately, the hope is that Walmex replicates parentco Walmart’s omnichannel success in the US, so the fact that Walmex gained a point of incremental e-commerce penetration in Q2 (8.8% of industry GMV) is an encouraging takeaway.

Margins Compress in Pursuit of Growth

The big miss and the key reason for Walmex’s selloff post-earnings was really its margin. Starting at the gross level, margins in Mexico dipped ~10bps to 24%, while on a consolidated basis (including Central America), Walmex saw its gross margin come in flat YoY at 24.1%. Given full-year guidance coming into earnings was for gross margin expansion, the market was rightly concerned.

A closer look, however, shows that the gross margin miss was really down to Walmex trading off prices for market share in Q2. To some extent, this has worked, as a widening price gap (~40bp differential vs competitors) did also drive sales momentum and market share. The issue, though, is that Walmex didn’t attract enough new incremental business to fully offset its price investments; hence, the below-par gross margin result.

It was a similar story at the operating level, as Walmex’s reinvestments into tech, new stores, and refurbishment led to a big increase in operating expenses. Now, the longer-term nature of these investments means returns will be negative for a while. Thus, Walmex didn’t have much cushion at all against external headwinds like the low-teens % minimum wage increase in Mexico and higher than usual core inflation (+4% YoY in April/May) that hit Q2; hence, the ~90bps YoY margin compression.

Backend Loaded Guide Adds Near-Term Risk

As disappointing as the margin numbers were, the biggest disappointment of all, in my view, was that management didn’t take the opportunity to reset its full-year guidance bar. Instead, they reiterated their guide—firstly, for +6-7% revenue growth and secondly, for gross margin expansion in 2025. On both counts, this implies an acceleration in the back half of the year (i.e., a ‘hockey stick’) to make their numbers.

This seems risky, in my view. Yes, sales could pick up if macro uncertainties (internal and external) settle down. But ultimately, this is out of management’s control, and Mexican consumer confidence may not recover as expected. Achieving gross margin expansion, as implied in the guide, will be even more challenging - especially if competition pushes Walmex to continue investing in price. In sum, not an ideal near-term setup—even if the company does still have a lot of fundamental strengths.

Summing Up

Walmex stock was rightly punished post-earnings; while the sales momentum was good, a lot of it was down to pricing, which in turn, led to a big margin trade-off. From here, making guidance will depend on the pace of a Mexican consumer and broader economic recovery, as well as the competitive response. None of these are sure bets, and thus, there’s a lot of near-term risk.

To be fair, Walmex’s premium to its US parent company has also flipped to a discount as a result of its difficulties. Still, at ~19x forward earnings, the stock isn’t as cheap as I’d like, nor is it priced for a worst-case scenario. Net-net, I won’t be chasing the Walmex dip just yet.

data.title: NANO Nuclear Energy: The Catalysts Keep Coming
data.description:
Introduction

I last covered NANO Nuclear Energy (NASDAQ:NNE) back in February, and since then, it has done fairly well, although the ride has been bumpy, as it was with most stocks back in April. It's a good thing I wrote this line in that article:

To that end, it has earned a buy rating from me, with the caveat that it is only suitable for the most aggressive investors, bordering on speculators.

From the date that article was published to now, we saw a 42% decline, but also a 14% return. Crazy stuff.

After all, the company still doesn't make any money at all. It's rare for me to look up a company's revenue on YCharts and get "No data to display."

This is because NNE is a special kind of company: "pre-revenue." This essentially means they have no sellable product. They do have some really cool 3D renders on their website, though.

Welcome to the SMR World

NNE is a "small modular reactor" company (among other things, see the next section), i.e., they are working to build tiny nuclear reactors. These are power stations that could be placed on a semi-truck. I'm not kidding, take a look at this thing. It's only a render, but it really blows my mind (as a finance guy, not a scientist)

The possibilities of these microreactors (1-20MW), as they're called when they're this small, are endless:

  • Emergency relief after a disaster takes a traditional power plant offline.
  • Remote settlements that cannot access traditional power grids.
  • Military bases that need self-sufficient, internally generated power.
  • Backup power for hospitals, data centers, factories, etc.

However, these are still theory. There is no product yet. SA Quant gives NNE its lowest grade in this category, and the numbers don't lie.

That being said, there is one positive thing to note here, and this is generally true among NNE's competitors too, they have a lot of cash on hand. A long cash runway is necessary for these kinds of ventures. They've been able to raise cash going into the new year, a good sign.

One of the reasons NNE is interesting to me in this sector is because its cash burn isn't so terrible, mostly because they've been working to reduce all of their other costs. They do this through their unique business model.

NNE Has a Vertical Business

As mentioned in my last article, I'm not a nuclear engineer, so I'll let NANO explain their second part of their business, HALEU, or high-assey low-enriched uranium.

HALEU is uranium that has been enriched so that the concentration of the fissile isotope U-235 is between 5 and 19.9 percent of the mass of the fuel (uranium-235 is the main fissile isotope that produces energy during a chain reaction.)

HALEU fuel has many advantages that improve reactor performance and is required for most U.S. advanced reactors to achieve smaller designs that get more power per unit of volume. HALEU will also allow developers to optimize their systems for longer life cores, increased efficiencies and better fuel utilization.

...

Through its subsidiary, HALEU Energy Fuel, NNE produces this substance, and is able to use it for their microreactors. This gives them a distinct advantage in the industry because it means they are not tethered to the same market price as their competitors, since it is able to subsidize the more profitable parts of its operations, producing power, with the extraction and refinement of the fuel.

Ultimately, this vertical integration is useful for NNE because the uranium mining industry is moving toward shortages, which makes any operation that can reduce its fuel costs highly valuable.

The Catalysts Keep Coming

There have been several major political catalysts this year in the nuclear energy space, notably the ascension of Chris Wright as US Energy Secretary. He comes from a nuclear energy background, having been on the board of Oklo Inc. (OKLO) before his new job in Washington.

The Trump administration sees nuclear energy as a means of reducing carbon output without the same costs that renewable energies often bring, and this could prove to be a boon for NNE and other nuclear-focused stocks.

Specifically, an executive order signed in May was directed at SMRs and microreactors specifically (causing NNE to jump over 30%), classifying the tech as critical for national security. While they were passed over in the first round of DOME testing by the government, so were essentially all other private sector firms. This one may take time to play out, just like the broader nuclear energy thesis.

One of the most recent catalysts that has driven attention to NNE is the completion of its ALIP system, which was a major milestone in proving their tech. They acquired the intellectual property a while back, but it has just now been completed and can begin testing.

Again, I'm not very knowledgeable about this field, so I'll let NANO explain:

By utilizing a time-varying magnetic field, ALIPs enable the movement of conductive fluids without mechanical components, reducing wear and maintenance requirements while increasing efficiency. Current efforts at the NANO Nuclear Demonstration Facility are geared towards the development of the first operational ALIP prototype and a custom designed test loop for research data collection, alongside a magnetic field mapping system, for the collection of manufacturing and performance data.

Future work will include testing and characterizing pump performance with different working fluids, simulating component failure scenarios, and material testing.

Any step closer to commercialization is a step in the right direction, as I see it. The market agrees, and this news pushed NNE up over 7% on July 23rd, when it was announced.

NNE Risks

Of course, this tech is likely to take another five years before it's ready to be fully commercially implemented, but that's often cited as a reason to invest now when the valuation is relatively low compared to what it will be once these are operational and NNE can actually start generating revenue.

If they start generating revenue.

This test looks promising from my view, and so do the microreactors, but all the information I've come across on these is from the company itself, which of course will paint a positive picture of their work.

There is still the issue of being pre-revenue, and it would behoove many investors interested in this space to consider the risks of the company actually going to zero if they burn through all their cash, or have to endlessly sell stock to raise more, which could dilute shareholders to a point where little value can be created for older shareholders.

The last stock sale included institutional investors, which is a positive, but shares were sold at a near-25% discount to market value, which is not a great look.

As mentioned before, this is a speculative stock, and a lot could go wrong here. Very little has to go wrong to derail most of NNE's plans, especially if the technology components fall through on their promises, and a lot has to go right for it to take off. However, the potential payout is huge if it all goes according to plan. After all, the company is sitting at a $1.4B market cap. Competitor like OKLO is at $9B, and SMR is at $12B. There is a lot of room to run still, even for other pre-revenue operations.

Conclusion

Ultimately, the catalysts continue to come for nuclear energy. Political catalysts aimed directly at microreactors and SMRs like NANO Nuclear Energy (NASDAQ:NNE) is working to produce have lifted the company significantly. Add to this the idiosyncratic catalysts of their operations moving forward, with ALIP testing underway, and HALEU operations chugging along to keep their costs down, NNE is uniquely positioned among many of the pre-revenue firms to continue gaining value, and fast, especially if things go right.

There is little safety net here, and I recommend folks consider managing their risk in one of two ways:

  • Consider a very small position, "taking big swings with a small bat," and remember that position sizing is often more important than security selection.
  • Consider a diversified ETF that includes NNE, like the NUKZ ETF, which I recently moved to from the URA ETF. While this fund holds NNE at less than 2%, it also owns many of its competitors, which could act as a good hedge in case NNE fails while the nuclear energy field thrives.

I am continuing to hold a very small position in NNE along several other names in the field, and a larger position in the NUKZ ETF. I am very bullish on this sector, despite the recent run-up. There is still more room to go from my view, but we must remember that companies like NNE and the nuclear energy field in general's success will be measured in years, not months or weeks.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: VONG Vs. GGUS: No Need To Switch
data.description:
Introduction

Some investors looking to stay growth-focused but less dependent on the Magnificent 7 might start looking at ETFs that invest beyond the S&P 500 universe. ETFs based on the Russell 1000 Growth index would be a logical starting point, though it is still heavily exposed to those seven stocks. Here I review two such ETFs that use different versions of that growth index, those being the:

  • Vanguard Russell 1000 Growth Index Fund ETF Shares (NASDAQ:VONG)
  • Goldman Sachs MarketBeta® Russell 1000 Growth Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:GGUS)

As will be explained later, I will include the Vanguard Russell 1000 ETF (VONE) when comparing the two growth ETFs.

While both growth ETFs are outperforming the full index as represented by VONE, I rate both a Hold as there are better choices in the Large-Cap Growth space.

Vanguard 1000 Growth ETF review

Seeking Alpha describes this ETF as:

Vanguard Russell 1000 Growth ETF is an exchange traded fund launched and managed by The Vanguard Group, Inc. It invests in public equity markets of the United States. The fund invests in stocks of companies operating across diversified sectors. The fund invests in growth stocks of large-cap companies. It seeks to track the performance of the Russell 1000 Growth Index, by using full replication technique. Vanguard Scottsdale Funds - Vanguard Russell 1000 Growth ETF was formed on September 20, 2010

VONG has $38B in assets under management, or AUM, and has fees of just 7 basis points [bps]. The TTM Yield is only .5%.

Index review

The index is important to review when it drives a passively managed ETF's holdings. This is the description FTSE Russell provides:

The Russell 1000® Growth Index measures the performance of the large-cap growth segment of the US equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with relatively higher price-to-book ratios, higher I/B/E/S forecast medium term (2 year) growth and higher sales per share historical growth (5 years). The Russell 1000® Growth Index is constructed to provide a comprehensive and unbiased barometer for the large-cap growth segment. The index is completely reconstituted annually to ensure new and growing equities are included and that the represented companies continue to reflect growth characteristics.

Source: Index.

Holdings review

Starting with sectors, the ETF is dominated to no surprise by Technology stocks, just under 60%! Add in the next largest allocation (Consumer Discretionary) and the two account for over 78% of the total portfolio weight!

The table also shows how the ETF weights compare to the index it tracks. The above data was from 6/30/25; the next day the ETF would have realigned with the index but that data was not available at the time of this writing.

Top holdings

These 20 stocks account for 71% of the portfolio of almost 400 stocks.

Distributions review

I guess with such a low yield, the fact the payouts have a low growth rate becomes less important as income is definitely not a reason to own this ETF!

Goldman Sachs MarketBeta® Russell 1000 Growth Equity ETF review

Seeking Alpha describes this ETF as:

The investment seeks to provide investment results that closely correspond, before fees and expenses, to the performance of the Russell 1000 Growth 40 Act Daily Capped Index. The fund seeks to achieve its investment objective by investing at least 80% of its assets in equity securities included in its underlying index, in depositary receipts representing equity securities included in its underlying index, and in underlying stocks in lieu of depositary receipts included in its underlying index. The index is designed to measure the performance of the large- and mid-capitalization growth segment of U.S. equity issuers, with a capping methodology. Benchmark: Russell 1000 Growth 40 Act Daily Cap USD. GGUS started in late 2023.

GGUS has just $291MM in AUM with fees of 12bps, still very reasonable. Here the TTM Yield is still a low .5%.

Index review

As stated above, understanding the index is important for a passively managed ETF, even when they can invest outside that index. Here is the definition Russell provides:

The Russell 1000® Growth 40 Act Daily Capped Index measures the performance of the Large Cap Growth segment of the US equity universe, with a capping methodology applied. Russell 1000 Growth 40 Act Daily Capped Index is a subset of the Russell 1000 Index and includes those Russell 1000® companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values based on a combination of their market cap and current index membership. The Russell 1000 Growth 40 Act Daily Capped Index has been created in consideration of the definition of a “Diversified Company” via section 5(b)(1) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 (40 Act). At the quarterly index reviews, all companies that have a weight greater than 4.5% in aggregate are no more than 22.5% of the index, and no individual company in the index has a weight greater than 22.5% of the index. The index is monitored daily to ensure that constituent weights do not breach 40 Act thresholds.

Source: Index

The major difference between the two Russel indices is that this one has cap-weight restrictions.

Holdings review

Again, looking at sectors, we see Technology (46%) and Consumer Discretionary (14%) on top, but here being only 60% of the portfolio, versus over 78% in VONG.

Notice here the ETF and Index are more in sync as the data is after the recent quarterly rebalancing.

Top holdings

Here the Top 20 stocks account for 66% out of the 380 stock portfolio, slightly less concentrated than the VONG ETF.

Distributions review

GGUS is too young to detect a long-term payout direction but again, income is not the purpose here.

Comparing ETFs

Above I mentioned that the VONE ETF would be included when comparing the article’s two ETFs. My reasoning is if the Growth ETFs do not add benefits like superior returns or better risk ratios than an unrestricted ETF investing the same otherwise, why own it!

GGUS holds the highest percentage in growth stocks, almost twice what the balanced VONE holds. While each uses the eR1000 index as a starting point, the growth focus of the other two ETFs shifts their weight up into large-cap stocks, with both having a WA market-cap twice or more that of VONG.

When comparing sectors, there is complete overlap between the two highest (in green) and partial for the two lowest (in red) allocations, including that Technology is the largest allocation in all three ETFs.

This is a good example why knowing the date of the data viewed is important. The data on the one Vanguard webpage was older than ETF's webpage on Vanguard. The above for the Vanguard ETFs is before the recent quarterly adjustment; GGUS is afterwards so that would affect the comparison.

Keeping the date fact in mind, the Top 10 stocks in all three are basically the same, only the order varies. Since the end of 2023 when GGUS started, VONG has the best CAGR; a good distance better than VONE.

That said, when risk, as measured by StdDev is taken into consideration, GGUS then has a slight edge over VONG. The fact that VONG and GGUS parallel each other is the high overlap: 97% of the same stocks and 84% overlap in weightings.

Conclusion

Here it pays to look beyond what I reviewed here and explains why both ETFs get just a Hold rating. Both of these ETFs rank in the middle of the Large-Cap Growth segment as determined by the Quant Ranking system. As the title stated, no reason to sell one to own the other, though possibly switching into one of the higher ranked ETFs is an open option.

Portfolio strategy

My portfolio building strategy is driven by my age, now over 70, and a very comfortable nest egg, a concept I outlined in a previous article (article link). That has evolved into both a conservative equity ratio and use of non-factored ETFs like the Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (article link). Then I look for factored ETFs like those reviewed here with the goal of adding Alpha to my performance. To get selected, they need to outperform their generic index equivalent, which is why the third ETF was included in the performance review section of this article. Here, both VONG and GGUS provided Alpha and did so with superior Sharpe and Sortino ratios than VONE but behind the ETF many investors use to measure other large-cap ETFs against, the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY).

Final thoughts

The iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF) would be another R1000 Growth ETF to consider, and another Seeking Alpha contributor rated it a Buy over GGUS (article link).

The other data that can differ between providers and even inside Vanguard itself is sector naming and security classification. The ETF webpages use Communion Services whereas the Advisor webpage calls it Telecommunications. The stocks assigned based on those descriptions must be very different as the percents (.5% vs 11%) are not close! Something to be aware of.

data.title: Opera: Low Valuation Creates Exceptional Risk-Reward Opportunity
data.description:
For me personally, Opera Limited (NASDAQ:OPRA) had flown under the radar for a long time. Despite explosive growth in the stock price, there had not been enough reason to buy the stock for the long term beyond speculation. However, now that the company has proven its ability to sustain double-digit revenue growth and has figured out its monetisation strategy, OPRA looks like a compelling investment. Therefore, I initiate the stock with a Buy rating.

Opera might become a winner in the transforming search and browser markets

Let's dive directly into the bullish thesis around Opera.

With the introduction of AI chat bots and agents, traditional search and browser markets have been slowly changing. In many cases, people have started to look for direct answers inside the AI conversations rather than for links to websites with information. Although from my point of view this trend might not become as prevalent as some analysts expect and the traditional way of search is not likely to disappear, there is no denying some transformation is happening.

This dynamic could become beneficial for smaller players, especially browser companies like Opera. While Google (GOOG) will inevitably adapt to the new ways people look for information online, it can still lose some browser market share in the process. This is likely why many AI companies like OpenAI are preparing to release their own browsers — the shifts in the market can create avenues for new products with different user experiences. Since Opera has started to experiment with AI integrations and general user experience a long time ago, the company might start gaining some market share in the near future.

Opera is also well positioned to partner with AI companies like OpenAI. Opera has introduced their first AI-focused browser with direct access to ChatGPT more than two years ago, and the company's own AI engine Aria is developed in collaboration with OpenAI. Since Opera has about 300 million users who generate vast amounts of data useful for training AI models, collaborations with AI companies can be beneficial to both sides. Therefore, there is even a chance that if OpenAI or Anthropic decide to create their own browsers, they might actually partner with Opera for a white label solution or opt for a tighter integration with Opera's existing products.

Opera's financials are exceptionally strong at the moment

Opera has also recently started to look solid from a financial point of view; the company seems to have figured out its monetization strategy. On a broader level, Opera has been able to sustain double-digit revenue growth in the last eleven quarters in a row. This track record is important for a software company, especially in a such competitive market as search. Opera has also exceeded earnings expectations significantly in each of these quarters, which shows the market continues to routinely underestimate the company.

From the strategic perspective, Opera's ad business is growing rapidly. This also means Opera can start profiting from the aforementioned developments in AI quicker than other potential players.

Regarding profitability, Opera has demonstrated a solid 20-25% EBITDA margin in the last three years. While cash flow generation could have been better or at least more stable, (more on that later) most fluctuations are explained by accounting nuances rather than any underlying business issues. (e.g. a significant increase in receivables) With more than $100 million in cash and equivalents, no debt, and positive cash flow, Opera should not face any financial risks in the short to medium term.

Additionally, Opera's cost structure looks prudent. Even though the company's operating expenses did increase significantly in the latest quarter, the jump was driven only by the cost of inventory sold due to scaling Opera Ads. In turn, with a 40% increase in revenue, Opera's marketing costs only went up by 16%, and its technology and platform fees decreased by more than 40%. If these dynamics continue and Opera manages to decrease the costs of ad inventory with bigger scale, the company will be able to improve its profitability profile even further.

OPRA's valuation is low, creating a favorable risk/reward opportunity

Regarding valuation, Opera's stock looks very appealing after a period of consolidation. Its forward P/E ratio is now at around 14.9, one of the lowest in the last two years. Considering a 3-year average EPS growth estimate of about 20%, we get a PEG ratio of about 0.75, well below the "golden" standard of 1 and surprisingly low compared to the software industry in general, where PEG ratios of 2-3 are common.

The stock looks even more undervalued when we look at the multiples compared to the industry. OPRA's valuation is 10-50% lower than sector medians, which suggests significant undervaluation. It seems that the market prices in a "doom and gloom" scenario for Opera, which, again, is surprising considering the company's solid performance lately.

If Opera manages to convince investors that its business model is sustainable in the long run, the stock might be revalued closer to the industry's average. In this case, OPRA's valuation can easily double. With an earnings growth of about 20% and a PEG ratio of 1.5-2, Opera can reach the level of $43.5-58 by the end of 2026 without becoming overvalued.

Ratings

Regarding ratings for OPRA, the stock is rated Buy by SA analysts and Strong Buy by the Wall Street. Seeking Alpha Quant gives OPRA a moderate Hold rating. However, its C rating for growth is driven primarily by the slowdown in the GAAP EPS which, again, comes mostly from accounting rather than actual problems with the business. Additionally, low revisions grade is based on a small sample of just 2 down revisions for OPRA's EPS.

This is absolutely not to say SA's rating is misleading; these nuances are tricky to take into account. Still, high grades for valuation, profitability, and momentum seem more important to me here.

Risks and negatives

Acknowledging some risks and negatives, the major concern here is still the stiff competition. As I mentioned above, AI companies might introduce their own browsers in the near future, not to mention Google is likely to continue adapting to the changing environment as well and will not give up its dominant market share easily. And Opera's number of users has already stagnated around 300 million in the last 2-3 years, so it might take significant effort for Opera to improve its position.

Additionally, Opera's revenue now depends heavily on ads and therefore consumer consumption. This type of business is influenced significantly by the general macroeconomic conditions, as advertisers can quickly shrink their budgets in times of economic hardships.

From a financial standpoint, Opera's free cash flow tends to fluctuate significantly. For instance, in the last three years, Opera's Q1 free cash flow went from $23.3 million in 2023 to $8.3 million in 2024 to $12 million in 2025. Even though in most cases the drops are explained by one-time expenses or accounting principles, such positions as cost of inventory sold still expand too quickly.

Additionally here, with a dividend yield of about 4.6%, Opera pays a significant share of its cash to shareholders. While some investors might appreciate that, high dividends mean lower financial flexibility, especially for a tech company that needs to invest in development of its technology.

Finally, the stock itself is very volatile. OPRA went from $4 at the end of 2022 to $28 in the middle of 2023 and has been fluctuating around $10-20 in the last three years. Not many investors, especially large institutions, can tolerate such levels of volatility. Therefore, there will be some pressure on OPRA until the stock demonstrates some stability.

Key takeaways

Overall, after its explosive growth in 2023 and a two-year consolidation, OPRA finally looks like a compelling investment. Opera is well positioned to profit from the shifts in the search and browser markets with the introduction of AI agents. With the monetization strategy figured out, solid history of double-digit revenue growth, and strong financial position, the risks seem to be limited.

The competition might intensify in the future and macroeconomic changes could affect the company's advertising business. However, despite these concerns, OPRA's low valuation leads to a favorable risk/reward opportunity. Therefore, I give OPRA a Buy rating.

data.title: The Trade War Has Been A Boon For Boeing
data.description:
Like many people, I've lost a lot of faith in Boeing. At times, I've been in the "if it's Boeing, I'm not going crowd." Far too often, it's felt like Boeing (NYSE:BA) had lost its way, allegedly putting bean counters in charge (more on that later) and cost-cutting efforts ahead of innovation and safety. I take no pleasure in writing that and even less pleasure in thinking that. Boeing has played a crucial role in America's history and aviation innovation, but missteps, and crucially, missteps that cost lives, have called the company's prospects in question not just in an investment sense but also in a moral sense.

Hopefully, Boeing's new leadership can address the moral failings and restore Boeing to its former glory as an engineering and technology powerhouse. On the investment front, President Trump and his ongoing trade wars are turning out to be a massive boon for the beleaguered company. Coupled with the fact that Boeing and Airbus are currently a duopoly and combined are still struggling to meet demand in a timely manner, BA should enjoy plenty of uplifting tailwinds. Of course, another serious stumble, like with the Boeing 737 MAX, could create immense pressure on Boeing.

Boeing is Key to the President's Reindustrialization Efforts

President Trump wants to revitalize American manufacturing. It just so happens that Boeing is one of America's largest and most important manufacturers. Boeing directly employs around 136,000 employees in the United States, and indirectly, many hundreds of thousands more. Initially, the emerging trade war was seen by some analysts as a threat. Should other countries retaliate with tariffs, the thinking goes, prices for Boeing aircraft will spike, and rising prices should mean lower demand.

Yet there's a major hangup with that argument: Since the only current viable competitive commercial aircraft manufacturers are COMAC and Airbus, and given that Airbus is facing a backlog of nearly 12 years, airliners have few options, and buying Boeing was already practically a necessity for countries and companies.

China's COMAC may one day disrupt the industry, but it's going to take time for them to reach scale. 150 planes per year is a laudable goal, and I suspect China will reach it. COMAC may have a bright future ahead, but it's going to start as a relatively small player. A thousand planes already ordered is impressive in and of itself, but pales in comparison to the +5,600 plane backlog that Boeing currently has.

For Boeing, the Trade Wars Give and Give Some More

Turns out, President Trump's trade wars are becoming quite the boon for Boeing. Many countries have found themselves in a tough position, needing to increase purchases from the United States in order to ease tensions. One of the more straightforward and beneficial ways to do so is to order Boeing commercial aircraft. In the globalized economy, commercial aircraft are crucial for facilitating travel, bringing in tourists, and more. Commercial aircraft are also big ticket items, and purchases, especially large orders, will create a major impact on trade metrics.

Vietnam was one of the quickest countries to engage with the Trump Administration, and one of their first moves was to announce that they'd purchase 50 Boeing aircraft in a deal worth $8 billion. Other major announced orders include:

  • Japan is ordering 100 Boeing aircraft.
  • Indonesia is ordering 50, most being B777s
  • Qatar is making a record-breaking order of 210 aircraft, with the total value of the deal approaching $100 billion (although some purchases appear to be optional).
  • Bahrain is ordering 12 commercial aircraft.
  • China initially paused delivery of Boeing aircraft, but deliveries have since resumed.

So far, we're talking about only a handful of deals. However, countries are just starting to ink deals with the Trump administration, and I suspect we'll see more Boeing orders as more deals are announced.

Granted, it is possible that many of these deals are actually orders that countries were going to make regardless of the trade war. Ordering aircraft is a decade-plus-long endeavor, and few alternatives mean many airliners will have to turn to Boeing regardless. Still, I believe that pressure from the Trump administration will, at the very least, get companies and airliners to make orders quickly and for orders to come in at the larger end of considerations. Further, even if countries and companies were already quietly mulling aircraft orders behind closed doors, public announcements could still send share prices rising.

Boeing Getting Back on Defense

Boeing has also been selected to design and build the upcoming F-47 air superiority aircraft, a sixth-generation jet that aims to take the U.S. military to new heights. Defense is a separate topic worth digging into, and I'll probably circle back to it at some point. For now, I simply want to put defense spending on the radar. Since Boeing is so crucial for the American economy and military, I suspect the company's woes will actually result in increased support from the defense sector.

BA Valuation and Fundamentals

As you can see below, Seeking Alpha analysts have been swaying back and forth between hold and sell ratings. Boeing's brand has taken so much damage and the company has suffered so many mishaps, in my opinion, that it's reasonable to give BA a hold. (I give it a buy.)

Seeking Alpha's quant tools also give BA a hold rating. Valuation and profitability are the biggest black marks among the company's grades. Personally, I think the F rating for BA's valuation is rather harsh given how expensive American stocks have become. That said, I do agree that BA's profitability is a major concern and needs to be monitored closely. The company's net income margin is currently deep in the red, -16.58% (Seeking Alpha data), and the other profitability measures are more or less just as unappealing.

The bad news doesn't stop there. Revenues have shrunk by ~9%, although the forward projection pegs growth above 7%. Boeing has $23.7 billion in cash and short-term investments but also current liabilities of ~$104 billion. Most of the liabilities ($61 billion) are for unearned revenue (meaning planes ordered and paid for but not yet delivered.)

As for valuation calculations, I turn to Alpha Spread's DCF calculator. BA's share prices started to slide in 2020, and so far, the company has yet to fully recover. Net income margins have also dipped into the red, but before BA's decline, the company's net income margins came in at nearly 11%. While it may take a while for Boeing to recover, I do believe they will get back to profitability, although patience may be needed. As such, I based my calculation on Boeing getting back to an 11% net income margin.

This may not happen right now, but I believe it'll prove possible. Using this calculation, we arrive at a DCF value of $223.78 compared to a present value of ~$238. Boeing still appears to be a bit overvalued, but the company is in striking distance. Crucially, I believe President Trump's efforts to push BA will pay off, and rising military spending to support the F-47 program will provide the needed tailwinds. As such, I'm not worried about BA being slightly overvalued right now and believe the company will grow into the valuation and then exceed it.

(I set the discount rate at 10% as I use that for most companies to make them easier to compare. The terminal growth value was set at 4%, which aligns with what we normally see in regards to global GDP growth.)

Boeing will release its next earnings report next week. During the last reporting, BA exceeded expectations, reporting an EPS loss of just 49 cents, compared to an estimated loss of $1.54. Besides the last quarter, however, BA has consistently missed estimates over the last several quarters. Current estimates call for a loss of $1.47. Estimates have been ticking down in recent weeks; however, I believe there's a fair chance BA will beat estimates again, although perhaps not as dramatically.

Why I Could Be Wrong

Boeing has made serious mistakes already, with the 737 MAX chief among them. It's hard to know the inner workings of a company if you're on the outside looking in. That said, there has been a lot of chatter suggesting that the merger with McDonnell Douglas and a shift towards chasing financial metrics and deemphasizing engineering are what led Boeing astray. While Boeing currently has questionable engineering capacity, for a long time the company was famed for its engineering.

Now, Boeing is apparently trying to get back to its roots (engineering), but that is far easier said than done. Meanwhile, China's COMAC seems to gradually be gaining steam. Especially after Boeing's misteps, many airliners and countries may be open to passing up Boeing for COMAC. Additionally, if future crashes point to Boeing as being at fault, it could quickly and immensely reduce confidence among potential customers.

Key Takeaway: The Trump Catalyst is Enough to Earn a Buy

Boeing is in a tough spot, but at the same time, it's too crucial for the United States to let it sink. Of course, that doesn't mean investors are fully protected. If BA went bankrupt, the government might reorganize and support the company in a fashion similar to the GM bailout back during the financial crisis. That said, this is a pretty extreme outcome and probably a last resort.

For now, I believe incoming orders due to the trade war will provide Boeing with the lift needed to get shares airbone again. Other catalysts, like military spending, could prove crucial as well.

data.title: GoPro: Meme Mania Or Turnaround?
data.description:
GoPro, Inc. (NASDAQ:GPRO) has seen its stock price go on a tear lately. The stock started the month of July at $.71 a share and has climbed to $1.54 a share as of July 23. This jump really all came in the past few days of trading as the stock was trading at $.78 on July 17. That is an increase of 98% in just 4 trading days. The stock hit a high of $2.37 on July 23rd before coming back down to close at $1.54. It has been credited to retail investors and being a meme stock. Similar to the pricing action we saw previously with GameStop Corp and AMC Entertainment Holdings Inc. Volume has been through the roof during the rally.

My question is if there are real catalysts to the rally? Does it have any legs? Or it is nothing more than a meme stock rally that will quickly crash back to earth. It has been a few years since I last covered GoPro. I think it warrants a deeper look at what is happening with the stock.

GoPro Financial Recap: Overestimations and Declines

When I last covered GoPro a couple of years ago I rated the stock a hold due to a lack of growth catalysts. My article was titled: “GoPro Needs a Growth Catalyst”. At that time GoPro was profitable and had decent cash flow. It was recovering from COVID and the company was at least making money. The company also had estimated that it would continue to grow, albeit slowly. Two years later the company still never found that growth catalyst. It never really tried to expand or grow its product lineup. It also did not move into the software space more as it seemed like it might at the time. It did add Premium+ subscription in February 2024, but it is an upgrade to the previously offered Premium subscription. It will increase the average spend but not a real growth driver.

The company's estimates from 2 years ago turned out to be ambitious in the end. It estimated that it would grow revenues to $1.1 billion in 2023 and expand from there in 2024 and 2025. The company earned just under estimates in 2023 with revenue of $1.05 billion, then in 2024 revenues fell to $801 million, a decrease of 20%. The company also estimated that gross margin would go from 30% up to 36% in 2024 and 40% in 2025. During 2024 the company had a gross margin of 33.8%. Quite a ways under their estimate. In Q1 2025 the company had a gross margin of 32%, a long way to go to get to that target of 40%. The company also went from cash flow positive to generating an operating loss of $67 million and $133 million during 2023 and 2024, respectively. The company was not able to drive growth like it expected, my assumption of needing a growth catalyst was correct. Although I was incorrect in that I did not see the company declining like it did.

Nearly every financial metric has continued to decrease over the last 2 years. Take a look at the financial results from 2022, 2023 and 2024.

The one area of growth that GoPro was experiencing was in its subscriptions and services revenue. This had been a key driver of growth and profitability for the company when I last reviewed it. At the end of Q1 2023 the company earned $22.9 million from subscriptions. Q1 2025 had subscription revenue of $27 million. That line of business still saw growth, 18% over two years is not really strong growth, but it is still growth in the midst of a declining business overall.

GPRO Stock Price Pop Reasoning

The business has been in a negative decline. Why all the sudden has it popped? I want to review a few reasons that might have moved the stock price.

  • Oversold

GoPro stock price had seen a large decline leading up to the recent stock spike. The stock started 2024 at $3.42 a share. It ended the year at $1.12. It proceeded to fall in 2025 hitting a low of $.40 a share this year. The declining business that can be seen in the financials is the cause for that price decline. I have not been following the stock too closely, but at the lows the stock might have been worth a good look. Even a struggling business is a good buy at the right price point. Part of the price increase might be that it reached a point where it was worth a buy. Some of that price increase was justified based upon the stock being oversold. At the low of $.4 the company would have a market cap of around $56 million. The brand name for GoPro at some point becomes worth the market cap. It is still a strong brand name in the marketplace and the leader in the space. The company does have a good balance sheet with minimal debt (mostly convertible notes). In fact, during the conference call the company estimates that by the end of 2025 it will have $75 million in cash and $0 debt. Now we can take the estimates with a grain of salt since we have seen the misses on the prior estimates. That being said these are estimates for the current year and more likely to be reached. Really what I am saying is that even though the company has been underperforming, it appears that the stock price reached a point of being undervalued. This I think was likely catalyst 1 for the stock price increase, at least some increase.

  • Turnaround?

Is the company seeing its fortunes turnaround? That could be a cause for a stock price jump. In Q1 the company saw revenue decline from $155 million to $134 million, a drop of 13.5%. We already noted the poor gross margin, although the company noted that the gross margin would have been 35.5% during the quarter except for a one time sell off of old inventory. They expect the margin to be at the 35.5% in Q2. Revenue is estimated to decline by 22% in Q2 as well. So the bleeding is not stopping on the revenue side, at least not yet.

The company has taken steps to become more efficient as a company and return to cash flow positive. The company began cost cutting measures that are starting to show. Operating expenses were $62 million which is a decline of 26% year-over-year. It estimates Q2 operating expenses to be $60 million, a continued decline for the company and a drop of 36%. They are reducing costs faster than revenue is declining. This decline in expenses is expected to translate to savings of $100 million for 2025, bringing operating expenses to $240-$250 million.

It is also doing work on its inventory channels to improve cash flows. It expects to draw down inventories by $20 million and increase their cash, net of debt, by $25 million in Q2.

The company expects overall revenue and units sold to decline in 2025 from 2024. It does expect the subscription business to have small growth. It is working on launching a few new products, or new versions. The company launched the refreshed MAX camera, hoping to recapture share in the 360 market and setting the stage for MAX2 later in the year. They released a new Anamorphic Lens for the HERO13. The biggest potential launch is the tech-enabled motorcycle helmet. The company kicked off a joint venture with AGV.

The company has shown that they are becoming a leaner, more efficient organization. This is showing up on the financial statement. This makes the company more valuable in the short term. The problem is that the company has continually reduced their size to be more efficient. This just means the company is continually shrinking. Great that they can become profitable again, but they are now a smaller company with less revenue. It is a slow death. My analysis from before still stands, the company needs a growth catalyst. Maybe the entry into the motorcycle space will drive some revenue, if it is going to then it is not estimated to do much during 2025 based upon the company forecast. The company is seeing a rebound in profitability and I think they will move in that direction. I don’t know if I would call it a turnaround at this point with a continued revenue decline.

  • Tariff Impact or Lack Thereof

In the world of pain from tariffs, GoPro sits in a good position. It noted that 0 of its cameras come from China. The company has diversified the camera production outside of China. The company expects total tariff impact of $8 million for the year, which is expected to be fully offset by a product increase of 5%. There should be no effect on the gross margin from tariffs. I don’t think this is the cause for the rebound, but once again this was a fear that pushed the stock lower that has now been alleviated.

  • Patent Infringement by Competitor

GoPro filed a complaint to the ITC last May accusing Insta360 of violating multiple patents. On July 11, 2025 the company announced that a judge of the International Trade Commission issued an “Initial Determination” that found Insta360 violated federal law by importing and selling products in the US that infringed on GoPro’s intellectual property. There are two patents that were considered to be infringed upon: the design of the Hero cameras and its Hypersmooth video stabilization technology. If you look at a picture of the two cameras side by side, it sure seems like a pretty direct copy cat.

This is just the initial determination and the ITC is expected to issue its Final Determination on November 10, 2025. This does not affect the sales of Insta360 products for the time being. The Initial Determination is not binding. If the Final Determination determines the same results then Insta360 will likely be barred from selling their products that infringe on the IP. That is unless a say is granted. Although these are not common. If Insta360 were forced to remove its product from the market then that would be a boost for the business of GoPro. There would be one less competitor on the market. Insta360 is a major competitor along with DJI. Whether through either the removal of a competitor or a settlement of some sort for a license the revenues could see a boost.

Also even after the Final Determination is issued then there will still be a lengthy legal battle where Insta360 will likely appeal the ruling. The process could take years to resolve itself. And the effects of that ruling may not be permanent for GoPro. Either way those results could take some time before they show up for the company. That is if the final determination is in the favor of GoPro.

The stock price saw no movement following this announcement. In fact the price was $.80 on July 11th on the day of the news and was still $.80 on July 18th. While this could have a positive effect on the company it does not seem that this triggered the rally. It could have factored in to some extent.

Conclusion

The stock price of GoPro has shot up over the past week of trading. It has been labeled a meme stock driven up by retail investors. I do think there were a few catalysts that moved the stock higher. The stock reached a point of being oversold. The company has improved its efficiency and is estimating to return to cash flow positive. It looks like it might win a patent infringement case against one of its biggest competitors. These are all positives for the company. These may have played a factor in the stock price increase. I do not think that these factors alone warrant the price increase. I would have to agree that it seems the stock price is being driven higher by the retail crowd.

I have always been a fan of GoPro products. I have used them for years and always been happy with the product. I wrote an analysis on the stock 2 years ago about how the company needed a growth catalyst. The company was stalling. I see it no different 2 years later. The company has seen revenues continually decline. The decline does not seem to be stopping, at least not in 2025. The company estimates it will return to growth in 2026. That is a big question mark. The only potential revenue driver is their collaboration on a motorcycle helmet, which we do not have much information on at the moment. I am not interested in joining the stock price buying frenzy as I do not see the long term value in GoPro. That being said if the stock were to return to lows it saw before the rally then it might be worth a look.

data.title: Chinese Yuan And Australian Dollar Reach New 2025 Highs
data.description:
Overview

With a US-Japanese deal in hand, and the prospects of an extended tariff truce between the US and China, many perceive some tail risks associated with the US foreign economic policy have diminished. This has encouraged the animal spirits and helped drive equity prices higher. The dollar is mixed. The uptick in Australia's PMI and cautionary comments from the governor of the Reserve Bank of Australia have helped lift the Australian dollar to new highs for the year and put it atop the G10 performers today. On the other hand, a disappointing UK PMI pushed sterling down around 0.25% today and is the laggard among the G10 currencies. Emerging market currencies are mixed. Thai-Cambodia clashes weigh on the baht. The PBOC set the dollar's fix at a new low since last November, and the greenback recorded a new low for the year against the offshore yuan (below CNH7.15) before recovering.

Australia, South Korea, and India are exceptions in the rally in Asia, which saw the Nikkei extend yesterday's rally with another 1.6% gain today. The MSCI Asia Pacific Index was up about 0.8% today. Europe's Stoxx 600 gapped slightly higher today and is up about 0.65% after rising nearly 1.1% yesterday. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq futures are trading firmly. Bond markets are under pressure. In Europe, benchmark 10-year rates are mostly about three basis points higher, though the 10-year Gilt yield is nearly flat. While the 10-eyar JGB yields edged up to almost 1.60%, the yields on the 30- and 40-year JGBs soften a bit. The 10-year US Treasury yield is up a little more than a basis point to approach 4.40%. Gold reversed lower after reaching almost $3439 yesterday and settled slightly above $3387. It has been sold to nearly $3362 today. The week's low, set Monday, was around $3345. September WTI is recovering from yesterday's dip below $65 and is trying to find footing above $66 today. The 200-day moving average is slightly above $66.

USD: The Dollar Index overshot the (61.8%) retracement of this month's gain, seen near 97.35 to fall to a little below 97.20. It slipped to 97.10 today but is hovering around 97.40 in late European morning turnover. The US reports weekly jobless claims, which have fallen for the past five weeks, the longest decline since Aug-Sept 2022. However, next week's non-farm payroll report has more heft, and the early call is for job growth to have slowed (~115k vs. 147k in June) and for the unemployment rate to have risen (4.2% vs. 4.1%). Also, on tap today are new home sales, which are expected to stabilize after a heady 13.7% decline in May. The preliminary PMI is also due. The manufacturing PMI may slip for the first time since March. The services PMI may pare the 0.8% fall in June. The composite (output) PMI is seen easing for the second consecutive month.

Euro: The euro held above $1.0710 yesterday and ratcheted up to$1.1775, a 12-day high, helped by creeping optimism that a trade deal will be struck. It reached $1.1780 today before pulling back to almost $1.1745. The high set earlier this month was closer to $1.1830, which had not been seen since September 2021. The flash July PMI was slightly firmer sequentially, but the manufacturing sector continues to contract, albeit slowly (49.8 vs. 49.5). Yet the PMI has risen for the seventh consecutive month and has not been above 50 since Russia's invasion of Ukraine in early 2022. Services activity is improving. The 51.2 reading (50.5 in June) is the best since January. The composite edged higher for the second consecutive month, and at 51.0 (50.6 in June) matched the highest level since May 2024. It has held above 50 this year after 2024 finished at 49.6. The German economy appears to be struggling to sustain the upside momentum. The composite PMI slipped slightly (50.3 vs. 50.4 in June) after dipping to 48.5 in May, though the manufacturing and services PMI ticked up. The French PMIs, the manufacturing, services, and composite PMI, remain below 50. The French composite PMI has not been above 50 since last August (which was a one-month wonder). It stands at 49.6 (49.2 in June). The ECB meeting is as much of forgone conclusion as these things get. There is practically no chance of a change in policy with the deposit rate at 2%. ECB President Lagarde is unlikely to provide much forward guidance, but the market sees the central bank on hold until at least the end of the December meeting. The swaps market has about a 50% chance of a September cut and about a 65% chance of a cut at the end of the October meeting. Lastly, the EU-China summit today may be important in the bigger picture but does not seem impactful today.

CNY: Many, if not most observers, share our understanding that the PBOC's daily dollar fix is the chief way that Beijing manages the exchange rate. With Chinese exporters earning dollar revenue in a weakening dollar environment, it is not surprising that they are repatriating earnings and buying yuan. This leaves the banks as sellers of yuan and buyers of dollars. Yet, when some observers read media reports of state-owned banks buying dollars, they conclude it is "stealth intervention." We recognize two developments: First, the PBOC has adjusted the dollar's daily reference by a larger magnitude than at the start of the year. Second, since mid-April, the PBOC has been fairly steadily lowering the dollar's fix, and it is now the lowest since last November. Today's reference rate was set at CNY7.1385 (CNY7.1414 yesterday). The dollar was sold to a new low for the year against the offshore yuan near CNH7.1440 today before recovering to new session highs near CNH7.1545. The five-day moving average is slipping through the 20-day moving average. Note that the CNH7.1460 area corresponds to the (61.8%) retracement of the dollar's rally from last September's low (~CNH6.97) to April's high (~CNH7.43).

JPY: The dollar slipped in early North American turnover yesterday to almost JPY146.10. It eased below the 20-day moving average (~JPY146.20) for the first time in a couple of weeks. The mild follow-through dollar selling today met the (50%) retracement target of the rally from the July 1 low seen slightly below JPY145.95. The (61.8%) retracement is near JPY145.15. While many have focused on the trade agreement that brought down the reciprocal tariff and auto tariff to 15% from 25%, the agreement of $550 bln in direct investment is also significant. The details have not been published but consider that Japanese direct investment in the US tends to average $30-$40 bln a year. That includes retained earnings, while the new deal appears to envisage greenfield investment. And note that as of the end of 2024, Japan's stock of direct investment in the US was worth around $755 bln. The FDI commitment is around 18% of Japan's GDP. Meanwhile, the preliminary July composite PMI was steady at 51.5. The high for the year was seen in February at 52.0. The composite PMI was at 50.5 at the end of 2024. The manufacturing PMI rose above 50 in June for the first time since June 2024 but fell back below it in July (48.8). The services PMI jumped to 53.5 from 51.7. Its peak was also in February (53.7). Lastly, note that the swaps market is feeling more confident of a rate hike late this year. A little more than two weeks ago, the swaps market had about 10 bp of tightening discounted. It now stands at 21 bp, the most since early April.

GBP: Sterling rose for the third consecutive session yesterday and briefly traded to almost $1.3585. It reached the (50%) retracement target of the decline from the July 1 high. That retracement, near $1.3575, is above the 20-day moving average (~$1.3570). It has been turned back from almost $1.3590 today. Initial support is seen near $1.3515-20. The preliminary July composite PMI fell for the first time in three months to 51.0 from 52.0, which is the year's high.
